text: In the lateral decubitus position with the left side down, the surgeon explores the right diaphragm, revealing a hernia defect in the posterior aspect. A large amount of omentum and colon is visible in the right chest cavity, which is reduced without difficulty, exposing a seven by five centimeter hernia defect. The right chest cavity is fully visualized as there is no peritoneal hernia sac.

The surgeon mobilizes the right lobe of the liver, exposing the left lower aspect of the diaphragmatic defect through retroperitoneal dissection. The right kidney is reflected medially to enhance visibility of the defect.
The surgeon closes the diaphragmatic defect using figure-of-eight permanent sutures, starting from the medial side and progressing laterally towards the chest wall, utilizing an endostitch device.

The surgeon uses a suture passer to close the diaphragmatic defect laterally due to increased tension on the primary sutures. A coated propylene mesh is then placed over the entire defect and secured with tacks.
The surgeon allows the kidney and liver to return to their normal anatomical positions, which results in the majority of the previously placed mesh being covered by these organs.
